title: Add `podman container cleanup` to CLI
body:
When we run containers in detach mode, nothing cleans up the network stack or
the mount points.  This patch will tell conmon to execute the cleanup code when
the container exits.

It can also be called to attempt to cleanup previously running containers.

Signed-off-by: Daniel J Walsh <dwalsh@redhat.com>

comments.body: @mheon This is the first pass at the cleanup code to be called by conmon when detatched containers exit.  Probably need to store these commands in the database, since you could create a container and later start it, do you agree?
@giuseppe PTAL

@rhatdan Yes, these need to be in the database, definitely.
The difficulty here is going to be if someone runs `podman` with a non-standard store (changing storage driver, for example). We need to be smart enough to put that in the cleanup command.

@TomSweeneyRedHat Fixed up the man page.
@mheon @giuseppe @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL
One small nit on the man page, otherwise LGTM.  Would like a head nod from @mheon.
Sadly this is still not working correctly.  IE Conmon is not actually executing the cleanup.  Continuing to investigate.

this patch in conmon is needed: https://github.com/kubernetes-incubator/cri-o/pull/1641

I tested this PR with the patched conmon and it works well for me
@rhatdan Can we get a test that verifies that mounts are cleaned up with detached containers after this?
Yes I will add tests before merging.  Need updated conmon to make this work at all.

Tests are not happy.  For example, [from the smoketest][1]:

[1]: https://s3.amazonaws.com/aos-ci/ghprb/projectatomic/libpod/0fed8e6300ad8e3c09efeba81b77e0a9a91f6e2e.1.1530040259578152559/output.log

Oh.

Now that I think about it, this makes sense - we're running the cleanup code, which wants to delete the parent of `conmon`'s CGroup, in a program running in said CGroup, so naturally it fails as the CGroup is not empty.

I suppose we could request that we be placed in another CGroup as part of the entrypoint for `podman container cleanup`?

> Now that I think about it, this makes sense - we're running the cleanup code, which wants to delete the parent of conmon's CGroup, in a program running in said CGroup, so naturally it fails as the CGroup is not empty.

How are the control groups structured?  We should be able to leave any conmon groups alone and only remove *container* groups during cleanup, right?
@wking Right now, we create a shared parent for both container and `conmon`. This is what we're removing, as it won't be cleaned up when `conmon` and the container exit.

> Right now, we create a shared parent for both container and conmon. This is what we're removing, as it won't be cleaned up when conmon and the container exit.

So should we leave the shared parent and conmon groups around for an eventual `delete` call, and only cleanup the container group with the new `cleanup` command?

> I think this is one of the biggest bugs we have so we need to get this fixed this week.

Do we expect to get [the cgroup structure issue][1] (related to #820?) fixed this week?  If not, can we reroll this to only remove the container cgroup on `cleanup` and leave the `conmon` and parent cgroups alone?  That seems like it would be a fairly narrow pivot that would allow us to cleanup the other stuff at exit-time (although I haven't looked at the code, maybe it's not as narrow a pivot as I think ;).  And it would be one way to get this almost-working PR out the door this week.

[1]: https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/942#issuecomment-400461771

If conmon is in a cgroup and it launches podman container cleanup in that cgroup, how can podman container cleanup escape the cgroup?  Is there something that changed in the latest conmon to cause this?  The podman stop $CTR, were working prior to me vendoring in the latest conmon, I believe.
@giuseppe Any ideas?

> If conmon is in a cgroup and it launches podman container cleanup in that cgroup, how can podman container cleanup escape the cgroup?

I don't think it can, which is why I'd like it to ignore itts cgroup and only delete the sibling container cgroup.

Or just leave all the cgroups alone for this post-exit hook and cleanup the other resources.  Having one more cgroup directory (per controller) dangle until `delete` doesn't seem like a huge problem.

I think a better solution might be to undo our unified CGroup hierarchy work, and move back to a CRI-O esque model where containers and conmons have separate parents. This would actually remove the need for CGroup cleanup entirely, as `runc` at least can be trusted to clean up its own CGroups (I don't know if the spec mandates this, but it seems like a reasonable thing to expect even if not mandated).

> I think a better solution might be to undo our unified CGroup hierarchy work, and move back to a CRI-O esque model where containers and conmons have separate parents.

I agree, but don't know if we can get that out for this week's release.  Dropping cgroup cleanup here (deferring it to `delete`) seems like something we could get out.

> This would actually remove the need for CGroup cleanup entirely, as runc at least can be trusted to clean up its own CGroups (I don't know if the spec mandates this, but it seems like a reasonable thing to expect even if not mandated).

The spec wording behind that is [here][1].

[1]: https://github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/blame/v1.0.1/runtime.md#L133

@mheon @giuseppe @umohnani8 @wking @baude Cleanup passes.
`podman stop ` No longer cleans up, it relies on the container to clean itself up, which I think is the correct behaviour and fixes some nasty error messages.
Alright, this is gross but I guess there's nothing we can do to avoid it for now. LGTM.
